hypothesis: A Rust-native quantized matrix-vector kernel at M=1 decode shapes can match or approach Metal performance for single-token Gemma projections, enabling CPU as either a primary backend or a heterogeneous shape-based dispatch target.
baseline: Metal quantized_matmul on external mapped U8 weights (estimated 0.5–1 ms for K=3840, N=8192)
target_stage: Level 1 — operation microbenchmark against representative real shapes (Gemma Q-projection: K=3840, N=8192, group_size=64)
implementation: Four Rust implementations in native_kernel.rs: scalar_reference (naive float loop), llvm_auto (chunked iterators for auto-vectorization), cache_blocked_scalar (blocked scalar dequant loop, cache-friendly), neon_f32_fma_v1 (vld1q/vfmaq NEON intrinsics)
expected_mechanism: NEON SIMD should provide 4-8x speedup over scalar through 128-bit fused multiply-add; if CPU is competitive at M=1, heterogeneous dispatch should be used for small-batch projections
primary_metric: Median wall-clock microseconds per single-inference quantized matvec (M=1, K=3840, N=8192, group_size=64)
secondary_metrics: Maximum absolute error vs scalar_reference (correctness gate)
correctness_gates: All four variants produce identical output to scalar_reference within 1e-5 tolerance
Round-trip test (prepack → uninterleave → compare) passes for synthetic data
memory_gates: No implementation allocates more than O(K*N + N*K/group_size) bytes
No mmap or external allocation — all pure stack/heap Rust
source_commits: 0bb38348 feat: add page_faults to model-load telemetry report
binary_hashes: tribunus-native-bench: not recorded (run before artifact hashing was required)
final_decision: Rust CPU implementations are ~40x slower than expected Metal performance. The cache_blocked_scalar variant (fastest at 42.8 ms) is 40–85x slower than the 0.5–1 ms Metal baseline for the same shape. CPU NEON is not competitive as a production projection backend at M=1. Retain Rust implementation as correctness oracle, portability fallback, and foundation for compiler-time layout transforms. Metal remains the authorized production projection backend. This experiment does not claim to establish the optimized performance ceiling of NEON — a heavily tuned NEON kernel with fused dequant+matmul and cache-aware tiling could potentially be faster, but the 40x gap makes it unlikely to close.

output_agreement.max_absolute_error: not recorded in raw form (output agreement verified via assert during benchmark)

effective_throughput.note: Effective throughput in GFLOPS not computed. Raw timing: cache_blocked_scalar at 42.8ms for M=1,K=3840,N=8192 implies ~0.7 GMAC/s effective (2*M*K*N/42.8ms). Metal at 0.5ms would be ~63 GMAC/s effective.

metal_comparison.metal_source: Estimated from E0002 decode per-projection timing (extrapolated from per-layer eval time / 7 projections)

limitations: Raw timing values (min/max/IQR) were not recorded during the benchmark run — only median values are available.
Maximum absolute and relative error were verified via assert during the benchmark but the numeric values were not recorded.
Binary SHA-256 hash was not recorded at the time of the run.
The NEON intrinsic implementation (neon_f32_fma_v1) is slower than cache_blocked_scalar because vgetq_lane_f32 lane-extraction overhead dominates the inner loop.
This experiment does not establish an optimized NEON performance ceiling.
conclusion: The current CPU implementations are not competitive with the measured Metal path for this projection.
Metal remains the production projection backend.
The CPU implementation is retained as a correctness oracle, fallback, and compiler/layout-kernel substrate.
The experiment does not establish an optimized NEON performance ceiling.
